title: Rabbit pelt preparation and basic tanning
subtitle: Fleshing, drying, and the two simplest tanning methods for a small-scale operation.
excerpt: A freshly dispatched rabbit produces a pelt worth tanning if the skin is removed cleanly and processed within a few hours. The two practical methods for a small holding are brain tanning and alum tanning, both of which produce a usable leather without industrial chemistry. This tutorial covers the steps from skinning to finished pelt.
sourceNotes: Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, fur and hide chapters. William Vogt, Home Tanning of Leather (USDA Farmers Bulletin No. 1334, 1923), public domain. University of Wisconsin Extension, Small Farm Processing guides.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of removing any remaining fat and membrane from the inner surface of a pelt, using a blunt-edged tool while the pelt is stretched over a beam. Incomplete fleshing causes the pelt to rot in storage.

A method of tanning small hides using potassium or ammonium alum dissolved in water, sometimes with the addition of salt and an acid. Produces a white, relatively soft leather. Traditional in European fur processing.

A wooden or wire frame over which a fresh pelt is stretched flesh-side-out to dry evenly and maintain shape. Sized to fit the animal; rabbit pelts are typically stretched on a tapered board 30-40 cm long.

content.text: A rabbit pelt removed within an hour of dispatch and processed the same day produces the best results. Delay and refrigerator storage are possible, but the sooner the hide is fleshed and on a

After dispatch and paunching, cut around each ankle and down the inside of each rear leg to meet at the tail. Work the skin free from the legs and pull the pelt down over the body in a tube. Cut around the front legs and continue pulling to the head. Cut through the ears at the base and skin around the eyes and lips. The pelt should come free in one piece.
Turn the pelt flesh-side-out and drape it over a smooth rounded beam or post. Use a blunt tool (a piece of PVC pipe, a butter knife, or a commercial

tool) to scrape away any remaining fat and membrane, working from the centre outward. The flesh side should be clean and white-pink when done. Thorough fleshing is the most important step; any remaining fat will cause the pelt to slip and rot during tanning.

For a fur pelt that does not need to be supple (decorative use, craft work), stretch the fleshed pelt fur-side-in on a tapered board and leave to dry in a warm, well-ventilated space for 5-7 days. Once dry, the pelt can be stored flat. This method does not produce a washable leather but is the simplest process.
Option 2: alum tanning for a usable leather

Dissolve 250 g of potassium alum and 250 g of plain salt in 4 litres of warm water. Add the fresh fleshed pelt and work it in the solution for 20 minutes, then soak for 2-3 days, kneading the pelt twice daily. Remove, rinse lightly, and begin breaking the pelt: stretch and work it between your hands and over a stake repeatedly as it dries, for 30-60 minutes per day over 2-3 days.
produces a pale, moderately supple leather. The fur stays on. The finished pelt is not fully waterproof.
